basic_data.age: Mature adult warrior (exact age not given in source; presented as a seasoned, battle-hardened man in late 1851)
basic_data.origin: Caucasus; Avar Muslim from the mountain regions under Imam Shamil's imamate
basic_data.social_status: Naib (lieutenant) of Shamil, famed warrior and commander; at story open reduced from entourage of caracoling murids and banner to a hunted fugitive traveling with a single murid

physical_signature: Quick, restless black eyes scanning faces from beneath a hood, paired with a slight limp when he dismounts — the predator's gaze above a wounded gait, visible in every scene he enters.

psychology.want: To secure protection and military support from the Russians so he can return to the mountains, destroy Shamil, and liberate his family (wife, mother, son Yusuf) from Shamil's captivity in Vedeno.
psychology.need: To accept that no power — neither Shamil nor the Tsar — will grant him both honor and his family intact, and to reclaim his own moral center as an Avar man of faith rather than as a tool of rival empires; in short, to stop trading one master for another and stand, even alone, as himself.
psychology.fear: That his family — above all his son Yusuf — will be killed or maimed by Shamil while he is stranded between two worlds, rendering every compromise and humiliation meaningless.
psychology.contradiction: He is a man of ritual courtesy, Muslim observance, and deep tribal honor who nevertheless walks willingly into the camp of the infidel enemy, asking the Russian Tsar — whose soldiers burn his people's aouls — for help against his own Imam.

arc.start_state: A famed naib reduced overnight to a hunted fugitive; outwardly composed, inwardly calculating — still believing he can outmaneuver Shamil by allying with the Russians and that honor and family can both be saved.
arc.change: As Russian promises curdle into polite captivity and news from the mountains worsens, he grasps that he has been used as a political trophy, not aided as a warrior, and that every day in Russian hands is a day his son moves closer to the blade.
arc.end_state: Choosing action over waiting, he breaks from the Russians in a doomed attempt to rescue his family himself — dying in the fields as the man he was born to be (warrior, father, Avar), not as Shamil's naib nor as the Tsar's guest.
arc.arc_type: Fall (tragic) with flashes of Revelation — he dies knowing what he truly needed, but too late to live it.

voice_profile.voice_description: Hadji Murad speaks with the economy of a mountain warrior: short, measured sentences weighted with ritual courtesy and silence where other men would elaborate. His speech moves through formal Muslim-Caucasian greetings and proverbial turns, then drops into flat, practical statements about horses, weapons, routes, and men. When he reads that a host is withholding something, he does not press; he nods and the sentence ends.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Formal but unliterary; oral tradition rather than schooling. Caucasian Muslim register — Avar/Turkic greeting formulas ('Selaam aleikum', 'Ne habar?', 'May thy sons live!'), ritual blessings, and concrete martial/household nouns (rifle, sword, burka, saklya, murid, naib). Early-to-mid 19th century, pre-modern.
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Average 4–10 words. Declarative or brief interrogative. Often fragments ('My murid. Eldar is his name.'). Questions are short and instrumental ('Ne habar?'). Almost no subordinate clauses; complexity lives in what is left unsaid, not in syntax.
verbal_tics: Ritual openers: 'Selaam aleikum' before any substantive exchange
Blessing-formulas toward elders and women: 'May thy sons live'
The probing one-liner 'Ne habar?' ('What news?') as his real conversational opener once courtesies are done
Introducing his people by naming them plainly: 'My murid. Eldar is his name.'
Invocation of God as punctuation rather than theology ('Inshallah', 'Allah is great') at the hinge of a decision
avoidances: Never boasts of his exploits aloud — his reputation speaks, he does not
Never complains about physical pain, hunger, cold, or his limp
Never curses Shamil in another man's house; betrayal is shown by action, not vented in words
No sentimental or romantic declarations about his wife or mother — love is coded as duty and rescue, not confession
No ironic or joking register; no sarcasm aimed at hosts, no flippancy about faith
Never volunteers his plans; refuses to narrate his own strategy even when asked
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Feelings surface through body and ritual, not vocabulary. Anger tightens his sentences and shortens answers to one or two words. Grief is a silence, broken only by a prayer gesture — palms up, then drawn down the beard. Love is expressed as naming (his son, his murid, his horse) and as the insistence on rescue. When moved, he looks away, adjusts a weapon, or touches his beard; the line that follows is quieter and more courteous, not more emotive.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured, with pronounced pauses. Rhythm alternates between slow ceremonial openings and sudden clipped bursts of practical command; under threat it becomes shorter and drier, never faster.

dialog_examples.neutral_everyday: Selaam aleikum. Ne habar? — Your roof is well. The horse can stand here tonight.
dialog_examples.under_pressure: No. Not that road. We ride through the wood, before the moon. Eldar — the saddles.
dialog_examples.hopeful_joyful: May thy sons live, old father. If Allah wills, my son Yusuf will sit at such a hearth again, and eat from thy hand.
dialog_examples.angry_hurt: Shamil eats with the wolves now. I do not speak his name in thy house. Tell me only this: is my mother alive?
dialog_examples.vulnerable_honest: I have ridden with a banner and with fifty murids. Tonight I ride with one. A man learns late what he truly had — and later still, what he was willing to lose.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_Ne_habar: He already knows something is wrong; he is testing how much the other man will tell him unprompted.
subtext_rules.when_he_falls_silent_and_nods: He has registered a lie or a withholding and is filing it away; the conversation is over in his mind.
subtext_rules.when_he_invokes_Allah: He has decided. The religious formula is a lock on a choice already made, not an appeal for guidance.
subtext_rules.when_he_speaks_of_horses_or_weapons: He is really speaking of escape, of his family, or of the man across from him — the object stands in for the strategy.
subtext_rules.when_he_uses_elaborate_courtesy_with_Rus: He trusts them less, not more; politeness scales with suspicion.
subtext_rules.when_he_names_his_son_Yusuf: He is reminding himself, not his listener, why he must not break.

Shamil.quality: Shattered bond between naib and Imam; once warrior-brothers in jihad, now executioner and prey. Holds Hadji Murad's family as hostage-leverage.
Shamil.power_dynamic: Shamil holds the family and the moral authority of the imamate; Hadji Murad holds only his own skill, name, and the Russian card. Asymmetric in Shamil's favor so long as the family lives.
Shamil.speech_register_shift: Hadji Murad does not address Shamil directly on-page; when forced to name him to others he drops ritual courtesy and goes flat and clipped ('Shamil eats with the wolves'). Religious formulas around the name are withheld — a deliberate refusal of blessing.

Tsar Nicholas I / Russian command (Voron.quality: Official alliance dressed as hospitality; he is a prize, not a partner. Outward courtesy on both sides; mutual instrumentalization underneath.
Tsar Nicholas I / Russian command (Voron.power_dynamic: Russians hold territory, troops, and his physical freedom; he holds only intelligence value and prestige. The longer he waits, the weaker his leverage.
Tsar Nicholas I / Russian command (Voron.speech_register_shift: Hyper-formal, ceremonious register; longer ritual phrases, more honorifics, slower cadence. The courtesy becomes thicker as his distrust grows — a reliable warning sign to anyone who can read him.

Eldar (his murid).type: Master to disciple / commander to bodyguard
Eldar (his murid).quality: Absolute loyalty downward, protective authority upward. Eldar mirrors his disarming ritual exactly — Hadji Murad is a template as much as a leader.
Eldar (his murid).power_dynamic: Total hierarchical command, softened by shared danger; Eldar's life is in his hands, and his back is in Eldar's.
Eldar (his murid).speech_register_shift: Shortest, driest register of all. One- or two-word orders, names only, no blessings wasted. Introduces Eldar to others with two clipped sentences: 'My murid. Eldar is his name.'

Sado (host in Makhmet) and Sado's father.quality: Pre-arranged contact in hostile aoul; trust grounded in Caucasian hospitality code rather than politics. Recognizes Hadji Murad on sight and shelters him at risk to his own house.
Sado (host in Makhmet) and Sado's father.power_dynamic: Ritually equal under the laws of hospitality; practically Hadji Murad is the one who brings danger through the door, so the real debt flows from him to them.
Sado (host in Makhmet) and Sado's father.speech_register_shift: Full ceremonial register — 'Selaam aleikum', 'May thy sons live', shared prayer gesture. Softer, warmer rhythm than anywhere else; this is the only register where his courtesy is not also a weapon.

Sado's wife and the women of the househo.quality: Respectful distance governed by custom; ritualized blessings, no private exchange.
Sado's wife and the women of the househo.power_dynamic: He is the honored, dangerous guest; they are the keepers of the hearth whose dignity he must not compromise.
Sado's wife and the women of the househo.speech_register_shift: Formal blessing-formulas only ('May thy sons live'), no questions, no commands; voice lowered, eyes not held.

His family — mother, wife, son Yusuf (of.type: Blood bond / reason for every move
His family — mother, wife, son Yusuf (of.quality: The true north of the character; unseen but governing every decision. Source of his deepest fear and his only unconditional love.
His family — mother, wife, son Yusuf (of.power_dynamic: They are powerless in Shamil's hands; he is powerful everywhere except where they are. The relationship is the one place he is the weaker party.
His family — mother, wife, son Yusuf (of.speech_register_shift: Names are spoken rarely and quietly, never with adjectives. 'My son Yusuf' functions almost as prayer — the sentence around it tightens, slows, and usually ends there.

The hostile Chechen aoul Makhmet (collec.quality: Not a relationship of dialogue but of concealment; he moves through it hooded, off the main square, eyes in motion.
The hostile Chechen aoul Makhmet (collec.power_dynamic: Numerically and territorially against him; he survives by invisibility and by the one house that will open.
The hostile Chechen aoul Makhmet (collec.speech_register_shift: Near-silence. Only the minimum ritual greeting to passers-by if unavoidable; otherwise gesture, not speech — whip-handle, tongue-click, a nod.
